pages.1:
# 5- Vigilance

The purpose of this pilar is to avoid any possible harm to the users' interests, especially to exclude discriminatory biases.

# A. What are biases and where are they coming from?

Machine learning bias, also sometimes called algorithm bias or AI bias, is a phenomenon that occurs when an algorithm produces results that are systemically prejudiced due to erroneous assumptions in the machine learning process, mostly linked to the training data.

Discriminations due to bias in machine learning can take many forms - from racial bias and gender prejudice to recruiting inequity and age discrimination. We have seen some examples in Introduction.

The underlying reason for ML bias is not in the model equation but comes from our own prejudice - conscious or unconscious - transferred in ML models throughout their development, from the training data to what we choose to optimize. ML solutions learns from and scale human biases.

ML learns from the present to predict the future. Our society and humans being biased, one of the risk of AI is to put in production these biases.

## A.1. The main source of bias comes from the data

Here are the most common types of data or human bias that are learned by the algorithms.

· Reporting bias

This happens when the frequency of events in the training dataset doesn't accurately reflect reality. For example, if we take social media comments as the main data source, we often ignore the fact that only a certain category of people posts comments on those platforms.

· Selection bias

This occurs if training data is either unrepresentative or is selected without proper randomization. The classic example is facial recognition where we mostly see worse

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.2:
performance for women than men and for people of color compared to Caucasians. This is due to the lack of diversity in training data.

### · Group attribution bias

Group attribution bias takes place when data teams extrapolate what is true of individuals to entire groups the individual is or is not part of. This type of bias can be found in admission and recruiting tools that may favor the candidates who graduated from certain schools and show prejudice against those who didn't.

### · Implicit bias

This type of AI bias occurs when AI assumptions are made based on personal experience that doesn't necessarily apply more generally. For instance, if data scientists have picked up on cultural cues about women being housekeepers, they might struggle to connect women to influential roles in business despite their conscious belief in gender equality - an example echoing the story of Google Translation gender bias.

## B. What can we do?

The important message here is that there are no quick fixes. Some tools and technical solutions are being developed but there are not enough. Ensuring fairness requires a lot of conscious thinking, at every steps.

B.1. Understand the data and the model to assess where the risk of unfairness is high.

The first step is to examine the training dataset for whether it is representative and large enough to prevent common biases such as sampling bias and check if the different subpopulations are equally represented.

Then conduct subpopulation analysis that involves calculating model metrics for specific groups in the dataset. This can help determine if the model performance is identical across subpopulations or whether your model discriminate certain subpopulation.

Source: https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash-course/fairness/evaluating- for-bias

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.3:
Once in production, monitor the model over time against biases. The outcome of ML algorithms can change as they learn or as training data changes.

### B.2. Improve human-driven processes as you identify biases in training data

Model building and evaluation can highlight biases that have gone noticed for a long time. In the process of building AI models, companies can identify these biases and use this knowledge to understand the reasons for bias. Through training, process design and cultural changes, companies can improve the actual process to reduce bias.

Eliminating bias is a multidisciplinary strategy that consists of ethicists, social scientists, and experts who best understand the nuances of each application area in the process. Therefore, companies should seek to include such experts in their AI projects.

Diversify your organisation. Diversity in the Data Science community eases the identification of biases. People that first notice bias issues are mostly users who are from that specific minority community. Therefore, maintaining a diverse Data Science team can help you mitigate unwanted ML biases.

### B.3. Adopt technical solutions

There are many tools that can help you identify potential sources of bias and reveal the traits in the data that affects the discriminatory aspect of the model.

We also have certain mitigating solutions to counter act the biases.

Here is a non-exhaustive list of open-source toolboxes:

· FairML from the MIT: GitHub - adebayoj/fairml , FairML : ToolBox for diagnosing bias in predictive modeling )

· Fairness Measures Fairness Measures - Detecting Algorithmic Discrimination

· Themis-ML A fairness-aware machine learning library - themis-ml 0.0.2 documentation

· Fairness Comparison GitHub - algofairness/fairness-comparison: Comparing fairness-aware machine learning techniques.

· Aequitas GitHub - dssg/aequitas: Bias and Fairness Audit Toolkit

· Al Fairness 360 https://aif360.mybluemix.net/ - the one we are going to detail.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.4:
These technical solutions should be implemented at every step of the model development :

Source: https://www.ibm.com/blogs/research/2018/09/ai-fairness-360/

#### B.4. AI Fairness 360 (AIF360)

This is a toolbox first initiated by IBM, now a Linux Foundation incubation project, is an open-source library that 'can help users examine, report, and mitigate discrimination and bias in machine learning models throughout the Al application lifecycle'.

It is the most complete and commonly used toolbox (Home - AI Fairness 360 ) with 8 fairness metrics and 10 mitigation algorithms available (https://aif360.mybluemix.net/ ).

You can read their paper here: AI Fairness 360: An Extensible Toolkit for Detecting,...

They have published many tutorials and demos that you can use.

When you want to use fairness measures and mitigations, there are multiple steps to follow:

1. First, as mentioned before, you should understand your data!

2. Then you have to decide on what variable you will look for discrimination (called 'protected_attribute' in AIF360), it can be sex, ethnicity, age ...

3. Within this variable, you will define a privileged_group and an unprivileged_group (women/men, young/old ... )

4. Then you can compute your fairness metric on the original training dataset. There are many to choose from, we will give you a short explanation of each bellow.

5. If a bias is detected, you can apply a mitigation method (there are also many to choose from, more details bellow)

6. Re-test the fairness metric post-mitigation

### B.4.a. Fairness metrics

As mentioned, there are different fairness metrics to choose from:

Source: https://aif360.mybluemix.net/

All those metrics are interesting as they measure different aspects of groups differences, but the Disparate Impact is the most used metric to evaluate fairness. It

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.5:
compares the proportion of individuals that receive a positive output for two groups: an unprivileged group and a privileged group.

The calculation is the proportion of the unprivileged group that received the positive outcome divided by the proportion of the privileged group that received the positive outcome.

The industry standard is a four-fifths rule: if the unprivileged group receives a positive outcome less than 80% of their proportion of the privileged group, this is a disparate impact violation. However, you may decide to increase this for your business.

### B.4.b. Mitigation methods

Note: One approach for mitigating bias that some people often suggest is simply to remove the feature that should be protected. For example, if you are concerned of a model being sexist and you have gender available in your data set, remove it from the features passed to the machine learning algorithm. Unfortunately, this rarely fixes the problem.

Here are the different mitigation methods available in AIF360:

Different methods can be used at different moments of the model training: before (pre-processing methods affecting the data), during (in-processing methods affecting the classifier) and after training (post-processing methods affecting the predictions).

The choice among algorithm categories can partially be made based on our ability to intervene at different parts of a machine learning pipeline. If we are allowed to modify the training data, then pre-processing can be used. If we can change the learning algorithm, then in-processing can be used. If we can only treat the learned model as a black box without any ability to modify the training data or learning algorithm, then only post-processing can be used.

AIF360 recommends the earliest mediation category in the pipeline that we have access to and permission to apply because it gives the most flexibility and opportunity to correct bias as much as possible. If possible, all algorithms from all permissible categories should be tested because the ultimate performance depends on dataset characteristics: there is no one best algorithm independent of dataset.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.6:
Among pre-processing algorithms, reweighing only changes weights applied to training samples; it does not change any feature or label values. Therefore, it may be a preferred option in case the application does not allow for value changes. Disparate impact remover and optimized pre-processing yield modified datasets in the same space as the input training data, whereas LFR's pre-processed dataset is in a latent space. If the application requires transparency on the transformation, then disparate impact remover and optimized pre-processing may be preferred options. Moreover, optimized pre-processing addresses both group fairness and individual fairness.

Let's go into the details of the Disparate Impact Remover. It is a pre-processing technique that edits values, which will be used as features, to increase fairness between the groups. Disparate Impact Remover aims to remove this ability to distinguish between group membership.

The technique was introduced in the paper "Certifying and removing disparate impact" by Feldman et al. 2014 (Certifying and removing disparate impact )

The algorithm requires the user to specify a repair_level, this indicates how much you wish for the distributions of the groups to overlap.

Here is a visual summary of how to measure and mitigate bias in the model training pipeline:

Source: https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/ai-fairness/9781492077664/ch01.html

<!-- PageBreak -->

titles.title: # A. What are biases and where are they coming from?
## A.1. The main source of bias comes from the data
### B.2. Improve human-driven processes as you identify biases in training data
